lessons.title: DIRECTIONS
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Directions are instructions or guidelines that help individuals
navigate from one place to another or complete a specific task. They can be
given verbally, written down, or represented through symbols, maps, or
diagrams. Understanding and following directions is an essential skill in daily
activities: directions.
 Pair learners and introduce common phrases they can use when asking for directions.
Examples:
“Excuse me, how do I get to the bank?”
“Excuse” me, where is the nearest ATM or mobile money agent?”
"Excuse me, is there a market near here?”
 Explain that clear directions improve accessibility, prevent confusion, and save time,
especially in emergencies.
assessment: Project/Homework:
Assign a project-based task where learners create a simple map of their
town or community and indicate any key financial institutions, mobile
money agents, shops, and markets.
Learners to give an oral presentation on the map by describing how to
get to a bank, ATM, shop, or mobile money agent using proper

lessons.title: COMPREHENSION
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Comprehension is an active process of understanding spoken or
written information. It is important for real-life tasks, like following
instructions, having conversations, and interpreting information. This topic
focuses on building basic comprehension skills, especially listening. Learners
activities:  Explain to learners that listening requires concentration and the ability to filter main
ideas from supporting details.
 Read a story or informational passage.
 Tell learners to listen without writing.
 Tell learners to focus on the speaker, maintain silence, and mentally note key ideas.
Model Passage
“Kalulu and Suntwe: The Wisdom of the Calabash Bank”
Long ago, in a quiet village nestled near the great Zambezi River, lived
assessment: Observe maximum participation in the activities.
Learners should demonstrate understanding of each learning activity
through the activities conducted.
Learners to fill in a table while the passage is being read.
Learners to answer comprehension questions based on the given
passage.

lessons.title: DESCRIPTIVE WRITING
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Descriptive writing is an essential skill in both personal and professional
contexts, as it allows individuals to vividly convey experiences, emotions, and
observations to others. In real life, effective descriptive writing helps people
communicate more clearly and persuasively, whether it is in creative writing,
activities:  Divide learners into groups and provide them with a descriptive passage to read.
Alternatively, read the description to the class.
The Banking System and the Importance of Saving Money
A bank is a place where people trust their money to be kept safe and
secure. As you walk inside, the cool, refreshing air greets you, and the
atmosphere is calm and organised. The floors gleam under the bright
fluorescent lights, reflecting the polished surfaces of the counters and
walls. The scent of freshly cleaned tiles fills the space, and the sound of
assessment:  Observe that every learner is able to identify descriptive words in texts.
 Learners to describe objects.
 Descriptive writing uses sensory details to create vivid mental images.
 Important features include structure (introduction, body, and conclusion) and
precise vocabulary.
 Practicing descriptive writing improves communication skills in everyday

lessons.title: CONTRAST
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Contrast is an important concept in the English language that helps
express differences between ideas, events, or situations. In writing and speaking,
contrast allows for clearer communication by highlighting opposing thoughts.
Understanding contrast helps one improve their sentence structure, express their
activities:  Introduce ‘but’ and ‘yet’ with examples and discuss their usage in sentences.
a. ‘But’ introduces an unexpected or opposite idea.
Example:
Mutinta is smart but she doesn’t spend her money wisely.”
b. ‘Yet’ is similar to ‘but’ but can imply a stronger contrast or a
surprising twist.
Example:
“He had all the money needed, yet he failed to buy school
assessment:  Learners construct sentences using ‘but’ and ‘yet’ correctly.
 Learners correctly punctuate sentences with contrast conjunctions.
 Learners explain why ‘but’ or ‘yet’ is used in a given sentence.
 Class Project: As an after-class activity, learners select ten best written sentences,
write them on the chart and display on the classroom wall.
 ‘But’ and ‘yet’ are used to show contrast between two ideas in a sentence.

lessons.title: SHORT MESSAGES
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Short messages are a quick and effective way to communicate
important information in everyday life. Whether sending reminders, invitations,
or urgent updates, short messages must be clear and to the point. In this topic,
learners will appreciate the purpose of short messages and how to write them
activities:  In pairs/groups, learners analyse different SMS samples displayed on a chart (or written
on the chalkboard) and identify their key features.
Examples:
i. LOL – Laugh out loud
ii. OMG - Oh my God
iii. BRB - Be Right Back
iv. TY - Thank You
v. IDK - I Don’t Know
assessment:  Learners compose clear and concise SMS messages.
 Learners use abbreviations and symbols appropriately.
 Learners differentiate between formal and informal SMS writing.
 Group Project/Homework: Learners to create a collection of SMS
messages for various real-life situations. These should be displayed on a
chart in the classroom. Learners should be encouraged to add new SMS

lessons.title: FIGURATIVE LANGUAGE
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Figurative language refers to the use of words in a way that deviates
from the conventional order and meaning in order to convey a hidden meaning.
These include similes, metaphors, hyperbole, personification, idioms, proverbs
and many more. This topic, shall look at proverbs.
activities:  Put Learners into small groups and ask them to do the following:
a. Discuss proverbs
b. Interpret their meanings
c. State the situations in which proverbs are applied.
Example: Honesty is the best policy
Meaning: It is always best to tell the truth, even if it is difficult.
Application: Use it when advising someone struggling with telling the truth.
iv. Group representatives to present their work and teacher to give guidance during
assessment:  Pair learners and provide them with proverbs to interpret.
 Give learners situations and ask them to supply proverbs that could be used.
 Proverbs are well known sayings that offer guidance or advice.
 They also declare a well-acknowledged fact or universal truth.
 Proverbs are often metaphorical.
 They are a collection of generations of wisdom and applies to life on Earth.

lessons.title: PERSUASIVE WRITING
topics: INTRODUCTION
Overview: Persuasive writing is a form of writing where the writer attempts to convince or
persuade the audience to adopt a particular point of view or take a specific action by
presenting logical reasoning, supporting evidence, and compelling arguments. It has the
goal of influencing the reader to adopt a certain opinion, ideas or complete an action.
activities:  Distribute a passage to learners and ask them to read it.
Example:
The Inclusion Revolution: Why Special Education Belongs in the
Mainstream Classroom
For decades, learners with disabilities have been segregated from their
non-disabled peers, relegated to special education classrooms that
resources: However, this approach is not only ineffective but also unjust. It is time
to revolutionise the way we approach special education, embracing
inclusive education as the norm.
One of the primary arguments against inclusive education is that it
would be too difficult for learners with disabilities to keep up with their
non-disabled peers. However, this assumption is rooted in a flawed
assessment:  Ask each group to write a composition using points gathered.
 Give homework for individual practice.
 Argumentative composition is a type of writing that aims to persuade or convince the
reader to adopt a particular point of view or accept a certain claim.
 It presents a claim or position.
 The writer supports the argument with evidence and reasoning.

lessons.title: CONDITION
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Conditional sentences are an important aspect of language that enable us to
express hypothetical or uncertain situations and their potential consequences. They also
allow us to address the question ‘what if’, which makes them an important tool for
critical thinking, problem solving and effective communication. Conditional sentences
activities:  Display a chart with three conditional sentences.
 Divide learners into groups and ask them to discuss the differences they note in given
conditional sentences.
Examples:
If you work hard, you will be successful.
If you worked hard, you would be successful.
If you had worked hard, you would have been successful.
 Explain the three types of conditional sentences.

assessment:  Learners distinguish types of conditionals.
 Learners match the conditional clause with the appropriate main clause.
 There are three types of conditional sentences.
 They express a condition and its corresponding consequence.
 They consist of two clauses; the if-clause (or condition) and the main clause (or
consequence).

lessons.title: FIGURATIVE LANGUAGE
topics: INTRODUCTION:
 Overview: Figurative language refers to the use of words in a way that deviates
from the conventional order and meaning in order to convey a hidden meaning.
These include similes, metaphors, hyperbole, personification, idioms, proverbs
and many more. This topic, shall look at riddles.
activities:  Explain riddles- questions, statements or short poems that are meant to be puzzling
or tricky.
Examples of riddles with answers
I am as clear as glass but polluted everyday by human actions, in a most harmful
way. What am I? Answer: water
leaving all behind. What am I? Answer: carbon dioxide.
I am a necessary process but often done with neglect. Leaving behind waste, a toxic
inspects. What am I? Answer: waste management
assessment:  Provide guided practice by giving learners a riddle to provide meaning in pairs or small
 Allow learners to share their riddles with the class.
 Assess participation by observing learners’ engagement and involvement in the activity.
(pair or group).
 Learners to conduct a project of collecting riddles common in their communities.
 Learners to interpret riddles.

convince or persuade the audience to adopt a particular point of view or take a specific
action by presenting logical reasoning, supporting evidence, and compelling arguments.
It has the goal of influencing the reader to adopt a certain opinion, ideas or complete an

lessons.title: ABILITY
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Ability refers to the capacity to perform a specific task or function. It can
also refer to a natural talent or skill that someone or something possesses.
Understanding ability is important in real-life situations because it helps individuals to
talk about skills in conversations, explain past experiences, ask for or offer help. This
activities:  Select a learner to perform a few tasks. (touch the board, close the window, ride a
bicycle, sing, spell a word, breath in, breath out
 Ask learners to use the word ‘can’ or ‘be able to’ to describe what their classmate is
able to do.
 Explain the different ways of expressing ability:
Can and be able to both express ability in the present but can is more
Note: Can and be able to should not be used together.
I can be able to come tomorrow. (Wrong)
assessment:  Learners discuss ways of expressing ability in the present, past and future.
 Learners construct sentences expressing ability using different expressions.
 Ability refers to capacity to perform a specific task or function.
 It can also refer to a natural talent or skill that someone or something possesses.
 Expressing ability in English can be done using various grammatical structures and
vocabulary.

lessons.title: NOTE SUMMARY
topics: INTRODUCTION:
 Overview: A note summary is a set of points or notes obtained from a passage or an
oral source. It is a collection of all the important points. These points are listed. The
points do not need to be written in full sentences. Abbreviations may also be used.
activities:  Pair learners and ask them to name sources of information.
Oral sources: Television, YOUTUBE videos, Radio, Speeches
Written sources: Newspapers, Articles, Journals, Magazines, Textbooks
 Explain that we take notes from an oral source, while we make notes from a written
source i.e. Note making and Note taking.
Note making is the process of writing down important information in a short and
organised form for future reference from a written source.
Note taking is the process of quickly taking down information as you listen to an oral
resources: major drivers of biodiversity loss. The consequences of biodiversity are far
reaching and can have significant impact on human well-being.
Use the following questions to make note summaries of the passage.
What is biodiversity?
Why is biodiversity important?
What are the benefits of biodiversity?
assessment:  Observe learners as lesson is progressing to monitor their progress and understanding of
note summary skills
 Learners to write note summaries by giving them individual exercise.
 There are two types of note summaries which are note- making and note taking.
 Note making entails making note from a written source whereas note-taking entails
taking note from an oral source.

lessons.title: DIARY WRITING
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Writing composition is about sharing your thoughts, ideas and stories in a
structured and creative way. It is not just about putting words together, but about
organising them to communicate effectively, entertain, inform or persuade. This topic
will explore diary writing.
activities: events, or experiences.
SUB-TOPIC: Introduction to Writing Diary Entries
 INTRODUCTION
Diary entries are a form of personal writing used to capture thoughts, feelings,
experiences, and daily events. A diary serves as a private space for self-expression and
reflection, often written in chronological order with dated entries. The diary helps
improve self-expression, emotional awareness and writing skills. Imagine reading your
diary years later and reliving your happiest moments, learning from your challenges, or
assessment: Homework
 Learners write a diary entry for the next few days and reflect on how it feels to maintain
a diary.
 Diary entries are a form of personal writing used to capture thoughts, feelings,
experiences, and daily events in real life.
 It serves as a private space for self-expression and reflection, often written in

lessons.title: THE GERUND AND THE PRESENT PARTICIPLE
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: The Gerund and the Present Participle look identical but they have different
grammatical functions. It is their identical nature that brings about confusion. This topic
therefore, describes the gerund and the present participle, distinguishing them and
discussing their functions.
activities:  Provide two sentences: one on gerunds and another on participles.
Examples:
Playing for a team is an honour. (Gerund)
He likes playing for the team. (Present participle)
 Ask learners to differentiate the meaning between the two sentences.
 Explain the difference between gerunds and participles.
 Explain functions of gerunds and participles.
a gerund is that form of the verb which ends in –ing, and functions as a noun in
assessment:  Learners identify gerunds and present participles in sentences.
 Learners use gerunds and present participles in dialogue.
 Learners construct sentences using gerunds or present participles.
 Gerunds and participles, though both derive from verbs and ending in -ing, have
distinct grammatical functions.
 Gerunds function as nouns while participles function as adjectives or part of a verb

lessons.title: SENSE RELATIONS
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Sense relations in grammar refer to the relationships between words based
on their meanings. These sense relationships are important because they help us
understand how words interact within a language’s semantic system.
activities:  Discuss synonyms and antonyms:
synonym is a word or a phrase that has the same or very similar meaning
to another word or phrase.
Example:
Fast – quick
Big - huge
antonym is a word that has the opposite meaning of another word.
Example:
assessment:  Learners identify synonyms and antonyms and write the activity in their books.
 Learners to fill in tables or supply synonyms and antonyms.
 Synonyms and antonyms are two key components of word relations.
 Mastering synonyms and antonyms helps expand vocabulary and enhance ability to
convey emotions, ideas and perspectives.
 Synonyms and antonyms are important in understanding language in real life

lessons.title: SUMMARY
topics: INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Summarising is a skill that is required for one purpose or another. A
summary is a brief and concise explanation or account of the main ideas or essential
points of a large text or event. A summary focuses on capturing the core meaning while
omitting unnecessary details, making it easier to understand the gist of the content.
activities:  Ask learners why organising information is important in daily life
example: in school, business, research.
 Introduce tabulation as a method of organising information into rows and columns for
clarity and analysis.
 Pair learners and ask them to discuss how they make data easy to understand.
Example: a list of learners’ names and grades, class timetable
 Provide pairs with pre-made tables containing information
Examples: sales figures, survey responses.
